title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a man called Peter talking about volunteering at a science centre. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a man called Peter talking about volunteering at a science centre. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A man called Peter describes volunteering at a science centre.
extracts.audio_script: Hello. I’m Peter, the visitor manager at Old Mill Science Centre. Our centre demonstrates science through activities that people can try themselves. The name Old Mill leads many visitors to think the building once produced flour, but it was actually a [9]paper factory[/9]. The river provided energy for machinery, and parts of the old equipment can still be seen beside our entrance. We opened as a science centre twelve years ago, and the number of school visits has grown steadily since then. Volunteers are extremely important to our work.||We have displays on energy, sound, space and the human body. Visitors may spend time wherever they choose, but the exhibition which receives the greatest attention at present is about [10]renewable energy[/10], since it includes a wind turbine model and a device visitors turn by hand to produce electricity. People joining our volunteer team are never asked to explain displays immediately. Their first training session concentrates on [11]working with children[/11], including how to encourage young visitors to try activities without giving them the answers too quickly.||New volunteers begin in an area close to staff support. Rather than running experiments alone, they first help at the [12]welcome desk[/12], handing out maps and telling families when demonstrations will start. Volunteers receive a T-shirt and badge. Because staff and helpers are often asked the time by school groups, we suggest wearing a [13]watch[/13]; it is more convenient than repeatedly checking a phone while speaking with visitors. Once trained, volunteers can help present simple experiments, always alongside a permanent member of staff.||We have recently created a special practical area for schools. Instead of another classroom with chairs in rows, it is a fully equipped [14]laboratory[/14], where small groups can test materials and measure results safely. Our next holiday event will take place there. Last year workshops about rockets were the first to fill; this time our most popular advance bookings are for the session on [15]robot building[/15]. Volunteers will help organise parts and ensure children follow instructions. No computer programming is required. Visitors who cannot attend in person are still able to take part: we are creating a set of short [16]video experiments[/16] for families to try at home with safe household materials.||Some students volunteer because they are considering teaching or science careers. After completing thirty hours with us, they may request a written [17]progress report[/17] describing their duties and strengths. Finally, our main fundraising project this year will improve the outdoor area beside the river. We already have picnic tables, but would like to install a [18]weather station[/18] that visitors can use to compare temperature, rainfall and wind speed over time. Our sponsored science quiz in June will support this purchase. Details of volunteering and the quiz are included in the leaflets by the exit.

dialogue.speaker: peter
dialogue.text: Hello. I’m Peter, the visitor manager at Old Mill Science Centre. Our centre demonstrates science through activities that people can try themselves. The name Old Mill leads many visitors to think the building once produced flour, but it was actually a paper factory. The river provided energy for machinery, and parts of the old equipment can still be seen beside our entrance. We opened as a science centre twelve years ago, and the number of school visits has grown steadily since then. Volunteers are extremely important to our work.||We have displays on energy, sound, space and the human body. Visitors may spend time wherever they choose, but the exhibition which receives the greatest attention at present is about renewable energy, since it includes a wind turbine model and a device visitors turn by hand to produce electricity. People joining our volunteer team are never asked to explain displays immediately. Their first training session concentrates on working with children, including how to encourage young visitors to try activities without giving them the answers too quickly.||New volunteers begin in an area close to staff support. Rather than running experiments alone, they first help at the welcome desk, handing out maps and telling families when demonstrations will start. Volunteers receive a T-shirt and badge. Because staff and helpers are often asked the time by school groups, we suggest wearing a watch; it is more convenient than repeatedly checking a phone while speaking with visitors. Once trained, volunteers can help present simple experiments, always alongside a permanent member of staff.||We have recently created a special practical area for schools. Instead of another classroom with chairs in rows, it is a fully equipped laboratory, where small groups can test materials and measure results safely. Our next holiday event will take place there. Last year workshops about rockets were the first to fill; this time our most popular advance bookings are for the session on robot building. Volunteers will help organise parts and ensure children follow instructions. No computer programming is required. Visitors who cannot attend in person are still able to take part: we are creating a set of short video experiments for families to try at home with safe household materials.||Some students volunteer because they are considering teaching or science careers. After completing thirty hours with us, they may request a written progress report describing their duties and strengths. Finally, our main fundraising project this year will improve the outdoor area beside the river. We already have picnic tables, but would like to install a weather station that visitors can use to compare temperature, rainfall and wind speed over time. Our sponsored science quiz in June will support this purchase. Details of volunteering and the quiz are included in the leaflets by the exit.

questions.question: The science centre is located in what was once a (9) .
questions.explanation: Peter says the building was previously a 'paper factory'.

questions.question: The current exhibition attracting most visitors is about (10) .
questions.explanation: Peter identifies 'renewable energy' as the most popular exhibition.

questions.question: A volunteer's initial training focuses on (11) .
questions.explanation: Peter says initial training concentrates on 'working with children'.

questions.question: New volunteers usually start by helping at the (12) .
questions.explanation: Peter explains that newcomers begin at the 'welcome desk'.

questions.question: Peter advises volunteers to wear a (13) .
questions.explanation: Peter recommends wearing a 'watch' for convenience.

questions.question: School groups now have access to a purpose-built (14) .
questions.explanation: Peter says schools can use a new 'laboratory'.

questions.question: The holiday session currently booking fastest is on (15) .
questions.explanation: Peter states that 'robot building' has the most advance bookings.

questions.question: Families at home will be able to watch short (16) .
questions.explanation: Peter announces short 'video experiments' for families.

questions.question: After thirty hours, students can ask for a written (17) .
questions.explanation: Peter offers a 'progress report' after thirty volunteer hours.

questions.question: Fundraising this year will pay for a (18) outside.
questions.explanation: Peter says fundraising will purchase a 'weather station'.
